### wildperoxin — 2019-08-09T23:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal.24002/post-275762>

From the 23andme test, my maternal haplogroup is L5c2.

Leaving a remider here to share a more in-depth health and general history.

\--

Thanks all! The cattarh stopped today. Well, at least decreased by 90% or more. This coincided with having a TCM session, so maybe that contributed. There's that.

I noticed it was mainly after eating or drinking. I got mild, barely noticable heartburn once every 2-3 weeks (since June). Also a feeling of pressure or something in my left ear.

\--

We're turning Wi-Fi routers off around here. The siblings are away. This is a huge step and so happy. People are seeing my health improve around here and I'm sounding less crazy to them, I think.

I can't wait until my wages come in to get ethernet switches, and look into an EMF metre, and so on. To be honest, my greatest impulse is to fly myself off to the tropics. Even if it is just for 4 days, for the 4 days I manage my own time and can work from anywhere! I'm reminded of Sarah's story that [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) shared, where she got better in 3 days.

And, I remember and know firsthand the huge difference it has made for me and differences observed in others.

\---

I do CT baths 10-30 minutes daily now. That's because it sometimes led to fighting to get to the bathtub first. I might be able to do more over the next week or two. Daily sunrise as always, and stepping out to do more outside whenever possible, whatever it is I am doing.

Today, I had some raw Bangaladesh-sourced prawns from the market, lightly seared/steamed basa fish, pork bellies, streaky bacon and lamb and beef mince.

It was a food heaven today. I'm looking for some oysters, thinking about getting some from the fishmongers at the market.

I might have a chance to try a liver and algae supplement. If so, I'll let you know. I'm not overly enthusiastic. I'm just looking at the calender thinking 'How quickly can I get abroad to a more suitable place/environment, continuing with the protocols while there?'

Just pack an EMF metre, kiniki wear, candles and a red flashlight, and I'm set haha.

If I can do before and after Dutch Panels, that'd be really useful.

I think after being pregnant and with the changing environment these implants contraceptives are more detrimental (from experience - I've been ill 3 times since October, when I had the second one put in). I'll outline what happened and my experience more in a future post.

Again with the testing, I'd be interested in Dutch Panels before and after removing the contraceptive.

What's a good raw oysters/seafood to raw organ meat ratio for O6:O3 plus overall balance? Something to test, and find out here.

My whole workplace is flooded with compact fluorescent and LED lighting on candelabras and chandeliers, and these are one continuously day and night. Like one isn't enough, as if it isn't bright enough, and as if the lack of UV/IR situation isn't enough...

In theory it is possible that my parents get less sick/ill than us because they grew in a far more native environment, in a different location.

After reducing my exposure to LEDs during the day, I notice I feel better, or different. It definitely doesn't feel worse. It's just hard getting around in the dark or with minimal lighting sometimes, so working on that. For reasons of making sounder decisions and being able to flow with life, I'll put up with less compact lighting and electricity.

It's temporary until I get incandescent. I get out and see daylight. I need to adjust the blackout curtains too; so much work to do.

Because I don't want to keep replacing bulbs, I reasoned the next bulb that dies, especially if an IR or UV one, the money I would've used to replace it is going into savings.

The statement: "Who are you good for if you are not good enough for yourself?" makes me pause and consider: "Is it too rash to reprioritise and spend my next wages on a flight ticket?"

My family are aware so they won't be shocked! It's simultaneously exciting and sombre times.

I am fine thank you.
Thinking a lot about fatty acids.
We all know that DHA is very important, thanks to [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/), reinforcing its major role all the time.
What he is not addressing is that people with many or most neolithic diseases, when tested, show the excess of DHA in their blood.
With that eating food high in DHA and (unavoidable)(high EPA) content, seems to be less urgent if not counterproductive because that depresses highly important AA, which usually is already low.
But, two other fatty acids, oleic and palmitic must be addressed in our diet.

I suspect that there may be a historical aspect to all of this.
With the advent of industrialization, many people then farmhands, started working and living without sunlight, in factories, tight buildings, or coal mines. Rickets, tuberculosis, and skin cancer arrived.
Someone noted the benefits of cod liver oil. At first, it was about its vit D content. Then its course must have been distorted and vent into omega3 hype.
But really it must have been about vit D and oleic acid content not DHA& EPA.
To this day Patricia and Ed Kane think of DHA & EPA as a curse, stuff to reduce. Their patients are usually high in DHA.
My hypothesis.

[@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/)
[@Billybats](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20191/)

Using descriptions such as mufa or omega, clouds issue, and discussion gets harder to understand (at least for me).
So, when I see dr Boros saying that he eats pork fat and drinks olive oil, and also means that fatty acids give up hydrogen to create water, see chart, I am looking for common fats. That fat is oleic acid.
It also happens that oleic acid is a must-have in the brain and nervous system, and it looks like it is at any cost on this side of the brain-barrier, even depriving the other side. That makes blood test of fatty acid that much more significant.
That, to me, makes oleic acid a significant player — worth testing and tending to it.
Add to that proper amount of palmitic acid, and you may get your beta cells to work better and help in reducing your belly fat and fasting insulin.

### JanSz — 2019-10-28T14:48:37+0200

Not one fatty acid test is able to have DHA elevated over their high laboratory range.
The reason:
the upper laboratory range does not exist.
see their sample report
[https://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/nutreval-fmv-3000-sr.pdf](https://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/nutreval-fmv-3000-sr.pdf)
So: everybody is advised to eat more seafood and eat fish oil pills.

That is likely because someone has a financial interest in selling those pills.
Genova Diagnostics does lots of tests.

Look, even on sample report, oleic and palmitic acids are rather low.
\----------------------------------------------------
I am looking at Genova Diagnostic tests and Patricia Kane way of dealing with them, from before I came across [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) at MDA (MarksDailyApple) blog.
That was before Jack opened this board.
When I started asking him about fatty acids, he eventually referred me to Patricia Kane's work.
Patricia Kane does her tests at:
[https://www.kennedykrieger.org/pati...linical-services/peroxisomal-diseases-testing](https://www.kennedykrieger.org/patient-care/centers-and-programs/genetics-laboratories/clinical-services/peroxisomal-diseases-testing)
Looking at her sample tests, she does not do ratios, such as AA:EPA or other, but rather judge individual acids by where they place within their laboratory ranges. %Status tells how far individual fat placed from the mid-range.
\----------------------------------------------------
When this board was open, I asked people to post their fatty acids test results. Many did, many had high DHA and low AA.
\-------------------
At the minimum fatty acids should be tested.
But when tests are absent, and the person looks like is having some neolithic disease, my first move would not be to tell to increase seafood because he/she likely already has high DHA.
\-------------------
Patricia Kane developed what she calls PKProtocol, that attempts to get fatty acids back to normal.
It is described in her book (picture below).
There are twenty-three samples of her treatment, before and after.
\----------------------
I am assuming that Jack reserves his knowledge about fatty acids and peroxisomes for his own use in farm patients.

### JanSz — 2020-02-20T14:49:37+0200

Voss, Aquafina, and all other special waters are costly and not worth the money.
Only industrially processed water, where deuterium is partly removed, have any value worthy to talk about.

It is ok to drink Deuterium Depleted Water DDW.
But it is good to have an idea behind water's function in our bodies, any water.

We are built from mostly water.
Think of our bodies as machinery with moving parts.
Water that is part of our body parts is made of oxygen and hydrogen, but it is not H2O.
The water that we drink is used for the lubrication of moving parts, similarly to oil lubricating car's engines .
Water that we drink, (H2O) is not used to build or repair body parts. It is used for lubrication only.
The normal, average volume of drinking water is about 2 liters/day up to about 2.5liters/day.
Drinking over 2.5 liters/day goes into a gray area, heading toward diabetes insipidus.

It is a good idea to wait for actual thirst before drinking.
That allows our bodies to work as designed. Thirst is one of the functions of a healthy body.
Numerically that means, drinking closer to 1 liter/day on average.
That will allow body to produce a little bit extra matrix water (that is later converted to H2O).
Allowing for the creation of that "little extra" comes with energy that is much more than energy naked person would receive on the equator, assuming permanent sunlight noon there.

# Thread: "CCO isn't the primary acceptor for NIR--mitochondrial bound water is"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cco-isnt-the-primary-acceptor-for-nir-mitochondrial-bound-water-is.24005/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Corey Nelson — 2019-07-30T18:25:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cco-isnt-the-primary-acceptor-for-nir-mitochondrial-bound-water-is.24005/post-275186>

Interesting work by one Andrei P. Sommer.

From [Mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase is not the primary acceptor for near infrared light—it is mitochondrial bound water: the principles of low-level light therapy](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6462613/):

"There is observational evidence that R-NIR photons and presumably other wavelengths (in model experiments we worked with 670 nm—a wavelength for which bulk water is practically transparent) interact with the bound water, i.e., nanoscopic interfacial water layers (IWL) attached to surfaces, especially to hydrophilic ones. IWL consist of 2–3 monolayers of water molecules. The interaction has at least two biologically important impacts: change in IWL density (volume expansion) and reduction in IWL viscosity"

Another: [Light Effect on Water Viscosity: Implication for ATP Biosynthesis](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep12029)

Might be the case that he's missing some key points about production of matrix water, structuring of water, and negative charge of EZ water, but I think the title of the first paper is at least a promising conceptual step .

To whoever's reading this though, is the friction/lubrication thesis suggesting _reductions_ in viscosity totally backwards, in your opinion?

### Jack Kruse — 2019-07-31T00:05:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cco-isnt-the-primary-acceptor-for-nir-mitochondrial-bound-water-is.24005/post-275226>

This is why 600-3100 nm light make energy and increase ATP.

No shocker.

It is also why UVA light and blue do the opposite.

That is why NO was innovated.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Jack Kruse — 2019-08-01T02:44:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cco-isnt-the-primary-acceptor-for-nir-mitochondrial-bound-water-is.24005/post-275295>

The first law of photobiology states that: a photon must be absorbed by some molecule within the tissue to have any biological effect. The identity of these chromophores has been the subject of much scientific investigation and speculation. Largely due to the efforts of Tiina Karu in Russia, the enzyme cytochrome c oxidase (CCO) has been identified as a major chromophore of red/NIR light (Karu, [1999](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0037); Karu & Kolyakov, [2005](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0038); Karu, Pyatibrat, & Afanasyeva, [2004a](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0039); Karu, Pyatibrat, & Kalendo, [2004b](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0041)). CCO is unit IV in the mitochondrial respiratory chain and has absorption peaks reaching well into the NIR spectral region (up to 900 nm) as well as in the red and blue regions. The most discussed hypothesis to explain exactly how photon absorption can stimulate the activity of CCO involves the photodissociation of inhibitory nitric oxide (NO) that can bind to the copper and heme centers in the enzyme and prevent oxygen from gaining access to the active sites (Lane, [2006](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0046)). In experimental models (such as isolated mitochondria) oxygen consumption and ATP production are increased and the mitochondrial membrane potential is raised (Passarella et al., [1984](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0070)).

A less appreciated mechanism involves light and heat‐gated ion channels. These cation ion channels are thought to be members of the transient receptor potential (TRP) superfamily, consisting of over 28 distinct members, organized into six subfamilies, based on their primary amino acid structures (Caterina & Pang, [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/jnr.24190#jnr24190-bib-0009)). TRPV (vanilloid sub‐family) members, including TRPV1 (capsaicin receptor), have shown to be activated by various wavelengths of light, including green, red, and NIR

### Sunny20152019 — 2019-07-30T22:12:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k-supplementation-in-newborns.24008/post-275203>

I am coming to the end of my pregnancy and I am deciding whether or not to do oral vitamin k supplementation for my newborn. We are not doing any injections/vaccines. An oral vitamin k supplement was suggested. My instinct says that if babies are born with low vitamin k levels, there has to be some type of physiological reason for it. Then I think what if babies born during those studies were affected by non-native emf and had low redox, requiring vitamin k. I am now not a believer in supplementation and the studies with the oral vitamin k resulted in a small percentage of infants with gallbladder issues. I am leaning towards opting out of the vitamin k supplementation and focus on increasing my vitamin k intake through food and to continue getting sunlight at the optimal times of the day.
Does anyone know a little more about this?
Thank you!!!

### Katie Durham — 2019-07-30T22:28:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k-supplementation-in-newborns.24008/post-275209>

Hi Sunny, I don't know anything about newborns.

I don't consider there to be any such thing as "Vitamin K", similar to there not being any such thing as "Vitamin B". They're both complexes. Although there is some overlap, K1 and K2 largely function differently. K1 is more involved in blood clotting and K2 in orchestrating what happens to calcium. And there is more than one kind of K2.

It doesn't all have to be within the diet because gut bacteria can also make the complex but maybe not enough. K1 is readily found in leafy green vegetables. K2 is much harder to include in your diet.

Just suggesting you make sure you understand which Vitamin K you need to focus on, or perhaps all of it.

### Katie Durham — 2019-08-05T00:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-k-supplementation-in-newborns.24008/post-275440>

Again, which K? K1 is easily met with leafy greens. Plenty of MK7 in natto. A little MK4 in pastured butter, cheese and some other animal products. You need all the Ks. MK4 is the most elusive. And whatever stimulates your gut microbes will help. Sunlight on your abdomen is an important part of that.
